'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyuto Onsenkyō]] in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita]]. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsurunoyu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurunoyu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|qq=|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring [[:en:Thatching|thatch]] and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|qq=|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]], a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR [[:en:Akita Shinkansen|Akita Shinkansen]] to [[:en:Tazawako Station|Tazawako Station]], then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From [[:en:Japan National Route 341|Route 341 (Japan)]], take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|qq=|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

== Nearby Attractions ==
* [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyūtō Onsen]]
**** {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|qq=|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}}
**** {{ill|Ipponmatsu Onsen|ja|一本松温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipponmatsu Onsen|qq=|1=Ipponmatsu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352778}}
**** {{ill|Kaniba Onsen|ja|蟹場温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaniba Onsen|qq=|1=Ganiba Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625863}}
**** {{ill|Ōgama Onsen|ja|大釜温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgama Onsen|qq=|1=Ōbuka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439784}}
**** {{ill|Tsurunoyu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurunoyu Onsen|qq=}}

'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyuto Onsenkyō]] in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita]]. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsurunoyu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurunoyu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring [[:en:Thatching|thatch]] and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]], a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR [[:en:Akita Shinkansen|Akita Shinkansen]] to [[:en:Tazawako Station|Tazawako Station]], then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From [[:en:Japan National Route 341|Route 341 (Japan)]], take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

== Nearby Attractions ==
* [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyūtō Onsen]]
**** {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}}
**** {{ill|Ipponmatsu Onsen|ja|一本松温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipponmatsu Onsen|1=Ipponmatsu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352778}}
**** {{ill|Kaniba Onsen|ja|蟹場温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaniba Onsen|1=Ganiba Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625863}}
**** {{ill|Ōgama Onsen|ja|大釜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgama Onsen|1=Ōbuka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439784}}
**** {{ill|Tsurunoyu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurunoyu Onsen|qq=}}

Its rustic appearance, featuring [[:en:Thatching|thatch]] and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]], a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR [[:en:Akita Shinkansen|Akita Shinkansen]] to [[:en:Tazawako Station|Tazawako Station]], then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From [[:en:Japan National Route 341|Route 341 (Japan)]], take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

**** {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}}

'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyuto Onsenkyō]] in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita]]. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsurunoyu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurunoyu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring [[:en:Thatching|thatch]] and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]], a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

* Railway
**** Take the JR [[:en:Akita Shinkansen|Akita Shinkansen]] to [[:en:Tazawako Station|Tazawako Station]], then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car

'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyuto Onsenkyō]] in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita]]. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsurunoyu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurunoyu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring [[:en:Thatching|thatch]] and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]], a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR [[:en:Akita Shinkansen|Akita Shinkansen]] to [[:en:Tazawako Station|Tazawako Station]], then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From [[:en:Japan National Route 341|Route 341 (Japan)]], take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

== Nearby Attractions ==
* [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyūtō Onsen]]
**** {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}}
**** {{ill|Ipponmatsu Onsen|ja|一本松温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipponmatsu Onsen|1=Ipponmatsu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352778}}
**** {{ill|Kaniba Onsen|ja|蟹場温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaniba Onsen|1=Ganiba Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625863}}
**** {{ill|Ōgama Onsen|ja|大釜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgama Onsen|1=Ōbuka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439784}}
**** {{ill|Tsurunoyu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurunoyu Onsen|qq=}}

He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR [[:en:Akita Shinkansen|Akita Shinkansen]] to [[:en:Tazawako Station|Tazawako Station]], then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From [[:en:Japan National Route 341|Route 341 (Japan)]], take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

**** {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}}

'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyuto Onsenkyō]] in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita]]. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsurunoyu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurunoyu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring [[:en:Thatching|thatch]] and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]], a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR [[:en:Akita Shinkansen|Akita Shinkansen]] to [[:en:Tazawako Station|Tazawako Station]], then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From [[:en:Japan National Route 341|Route 341 (Japan)]], take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

== Nearby Attractions ==
* [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyūtō Onsen]]
**** {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}}
**** {{ill|Ipponmatsu Onsen|ja|一本松温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipponmatsu Onsen|1=Ipponmatsu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352778}}
**** {{ill|Kaniba Onsen|ja|蟹場温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaniba Onsen|1=Ganiba Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625863}}
**** {{ill|Ōgama Onsen|ja|大釜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgama Onsen|1=Ōbuka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439784}}
**** {{ill|Tsurunoyu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurunoyu Onsen|qq=}}

'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyuto Onsenkyō]] in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita]]. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsuruno-yu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruno-yu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring [[:en:Thatching|thatch]] and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]], a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR [[:en:Akita Shinkansen|Akita Shinkansen]] to [[:en:Tazawako Station|Tazawako Station]], then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From [[:en:Japan National Route 341|Route 341 (Japan)]], take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

== Nearby Attractions ==
* [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyūtō Onsen]]
**** {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}}
**** {{ill|Ipponmatsu Onsen|ja|一本松温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipponmatsu Onsen|1=Ipponmatsu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352778}}
**** {{ill|Kaniba Onsen|ja|蟹場温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaniba Onsen|1=Ganiba Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625863}}
**** {{ill|Ōgama Onsen|ja|大釜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgama Onsen|1=Ōbuka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439784}}
**** {{ill|Tsuruno-yu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruno-yu Onsen|qq=}}

'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyuto Onsenkyō]] in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita]]. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsuruno-yu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruno-yu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring [[:en:Thatching|thatch]] and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]], a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR [[:en:Akita Shinkansen|Akita Shinkansen]] to [[:en:Tazawako Station|Tazawako Station]], then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From [[:en:Japan National Route 341|Route 341 (Japan)]], take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

== Nearby Attractions ==
* [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyūtō Onsen]]
**** {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}}
**** {{ill|Ipponmatsu Onsen|ja|一本松温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipponmatsu Onsen|1=Ipponmatsu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352778}}
**** {{ill|Kaniba Onsen|ja|蟹場温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaniba Onsen|1=Ganiba Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625863}}
**** {{ill|Ōgama Onsen|ja|大釜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgama Onsen|1=Ōbuka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439784}}
**** {{ill|Tsuruno-yu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruno-yu Onsen|qq=}}

Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring [[:en:Thatching|thatch]] and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]], a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR [[:en:Akita Shinkansen|Akita Shinkansen]] to [[:en:Tazawako Station|Tazawako Station]], then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From [[:en:Japan National Route 341|Route 341 (Japan)]], take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

**** {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}}
**** {{ill|Ipponmatsu Onsen|ja|一本松温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipponmatsu Onsen|1=Ipponmatsu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352778}}

'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] located in [[:en:Nyūtō Onsen|Nyuto Onsenkyō]] in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita]]. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsuruno-yu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruno-yu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]

Its rustic appearance, featuring [[:en:Thatching|thatch]] and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired [[:en:Yoshiharu Tsuge|Yoshiharu Tsuge]], a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. Wikidata name: Kuroyu Onsen
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{otheruses|{{ill|Semboku, Akita|en|Semboku, Akita|WD=Q846877|lt=Kuroyu Onsen in Semboku, Akita}}|The black-colored hot spring in {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta, Tokyo}}|Ōta’s Kuroyu Onsen}}
{{otheruses|Kuroyu Onsen (Kame no Yu) in Semboku, Akita|The mineral spring "Kame no Yu" in {{ill|Hakodate|en|Hakodate|WD=Q26418|lt=Hakodate, Hokkaido}}|{{ill|Mount Hakodate|en|Mount Hakodate|WD=Q3526214|lt=Hakodate Mountain}}#温泉}}
{{Infobox hot spring
| name = Kuroyu Onsen
| image = 雪の黒湯温泉Img354.jpg
| caption = Outdoor bath with a cedar bark roof {{Infobox mapframe|frame-width=300|zoom=9}}
| location = {{ill|Semboku, Akita|en|Semboku, Akita|WD=Q846877|lt=Semboku, Akita}}, Japan
| coordinates = {{Coord|39|48|9|N|140|48|21|E|type:landmark_region:JP-05|display=inline,title}}
| access = About 55 minutes by bus from {{ill|Tazawako Station|en|Tazawako Station|WD=Q692529|lt=Tazawako Station}} (Akita Shinkansen), followed by a 20-minute walk.
| hot spring type = {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
| external link = [https://kuroyu.com/ 黒湯温泉]
}}

'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} located in {{ill|Nyūtō Onsen|en|Nyūtō Onsen|WD=Q11370596|lt=Nyuto Onsenkyō}} in {{ill|Semboku, Akita|en|Semboku, Akita|WD=Q846877|lt=Semboku, Akita}}. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsuruno-yu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruno-yu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|WD=Q874531|lt=thatch}} and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired {{ill|Yoshiharu Tsuge|en|Yoshiharu Tsuge|WD=Q1353888|lt=Yoshiharu Tsuge}}, a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR {{ill|Akita Shinkansen|en|Akita Shinkansen|WD=Q220696|lt=Akita Shinkansen}} to {{ill|Tazawako Station|en|Tazawako Station|WD=Q692529|lt=Tazawako Station}}, then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From {{ill|Japan National Route 341|en|Japan National Route 341|WD=Q6157289|lt=Route 341 (Japan)}}, take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

== Nearby Attractions ==
* {{ill|Nyūtō Onsen|en|Nyūtō Onsen|WD=Q11370596|lt=Nyūtō Onsen}}
**** {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}}
**** {{ill|Ipponmatsu Onsen|ja|一本松温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ipponmatsu Onsen|1=Ipponmatsu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352778}}
**** {{ill|Kaniba Onsen|ja|蟹場温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaniba Onsen|1=Ganiba Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625863}}
**** {{ill|Ōgama Onsen|ja|大釜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgama Onsen|1=Ōbuka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439784}}
**** {{ill|Tsuruno-yu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruno-yu Onsen|qq=}}

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|WD=Q874531|lt=thatch}} and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired {{ill|Yoshiharu Tsuge|en|Yoshiharu Tsuge|WD=Q1353888|lt=Yoshiharu Tsuge}}, a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR {{ill|Akita Shinkansen|en|Akita Shinkansen|WD=Q220696|lt=Akita Shinkansen}} to {{ill|Tazawako Station|en|Tazawako Station|WD=Q692529|lt=Tazawako Station}}, then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From {{ill|Japan National Route 341|en|Japan National Route 341|WD=Q6157289|lt=Route 341 (Japan)}}, take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} located in {{ill|Nyūtō Onsen|en|Nyūtō Onsen|WD=Q11370596|lt=Nyuto Onsenkyō}} in {{ill|Semboku, Akita|en|Semboku, Akita|WD=Q846877|lt=Semboku, Akita}}. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsuruno-yu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruno-yu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|WD=Q874531|lt=thatch}} and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired {{ill|Yoshiharu Tsuge|en|Yoshiharu Tsuge|WD=Q1353888|lt=Yoshiharu Tsuge}}, a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

{{otheruses|{{ill|Semboku, Akita|en|Semboku, Akita|WD=Q846877|lt=Kuroyu Onsen in Semboku, Akita|1=Semboku, Akita}}|The black-colored hot spring in {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta, Tokyo|1=Ōta, Tokyo}}|Ōta’s Kuroyu Onsen}}
{{otheruses|Kuroyu Onsen (Kame no Yu) in Semboku, Akita|The mineral spring "Kame no Yu" in {{ill|Hakodate|en|Hakodate|WD=Q26418|lt=Hakodate, Hokkaido|1=Hakodate}}|{{ill|Mount Hakodate|en|Mount Hakodate|WD=Q3526214|lt=Hakodate Mountain|1=Mount Hakodate}}#温泉}}
{{Infobox hot spring
| name = Kuroyu Onsen
| image = 雪の黒湯温泉Img354.jpg
| caption = Outdoor bath with a cedar bark roof {{Infobox mapframe|frame-width=300|zoom=9}}
| location = {{ill|Semboku, Akita|en|Semboku, Akita|WD=Q846877|lt=Semboku, Akita|1=Semboku, Akita}}, Japan
| coordinates = {{Coord|39|48|9|N|140|48|21|E|type:landmark_region:JP-05|display=inline,title}}
| access = About 55 minutes by bus from {{ill|Tazawako Station|en|Tazawako Station|WD=Q692529|lt=Tazawako Station|1=Tazawako Station}} (Akita Shinkansen), followed by a 20-minute walk.
| hot spring type = {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
| external link = [https://kuroyu.com/ 黒湯温泉]
}}

'''Kuroyu Onsen''' (黒湯温泉) is a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}} located in {{ill|Nyūtō Onsen|en|Nyūtō Onsen|WD=Q11370596|lt=Nyuto Onsenkyō|1=Nyūtō Onsen}} in {{ill|Semboku, Akita|en|Semboku, Akita|WD=Q846877|lt=Semboku, Akita|1=Semboku, Akita}}. Situated upstream of the Sendatsu River, it lies at the highest elevation within Nyuto Onsenkyō, 800 meters above sea level. Until the mid-Meiji era, it was also known as Kame no Yu.<ref>谷沢明. "[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%BB%92%E6%B9%AF-1162374 黒湯[温泉]]". Revised edition, *World Encyclopedia*, Heibonsha. Retrieved November 21, 2024.</ref>

== Hot Spring Properties ==
* {{ill|Sulfur spring|ja|硫黄泉|1=Sulfur spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur spring|qq=}}
Despite its name "Kuroyu" (literally "Black Hot Spring"), the water is a bluish milky white.
[[File:Kuroyu Onsen 03.jpg|thumb|left|Milky white sulfur spring water]]
{{Clearleft}}

== History ==
The hot spring opened approximately 300 years ago, and its discovery is estimated to date back to 1674 (Enpō 2).<ref>[http://kuroyu.com/kuroyu/kuroyu.html Kuroyu Onsen Official Site]</ref> It has the second-longest history in the Nyuto Onsenkyō area after {{ill|Tsuruno-yu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruno-yu Onsen|qq=}} and was once nicknamed "Kame no Yu" in reference to Tsurunoyu. Historically, it served as a healing retreat for the {{ill|Akita Domain|ja|秋田藩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Domain|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 久保田藩|1=Kubota Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067720}}.

There are legends that the name "Kuroyu" (Black Hot Spring) originated either from the dark appearance of the water or from the belief that animals would come to the spring to heal their ailments. During the Boshin War, it was used as a military outpost, with soldiers from nearby villages stationed in simple straw huts for border defense. Before the war, an elderly caretaker, known for his sharp eyes, used to hang dried snakes under the eaves.<ref>Chiba Chihei. *Furusato Hakubutsushi*, p.57.</ref>

Although Tsurunoyu is now the most popular hot spring in the Nyuto Onsenkyō, Kuroyu enjoyed the highest popularity in earlier times. On October 19, 1967, it was designated a {{ill|National Hot Spring Health Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Hot Spring Health Resort|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}} as part of Nyuto Onsenkyō.

== Onsen Facilities ==
[[File:雪の黒湯温泉Img360.jpg|thumb|Wooden bathhouse with plank walls]]
Kuroyu Onsen operates as a single lodging facility.

Its rustic appearance, featuring {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|WD=Q874531|lt=thatch|1=Thatching}} and cedar bark roofs, outdoor baths, and bathhouses built using locally sourced wood, is a hallmark of its charm. The facilities include three indoor baths, two outdoor baths, and two waterfalls for therapeutic bathing.

Retaining its traditional role as a {{ill|Toji|ja|湯治|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|1=Thermal bath|2=ja|3=湯治|4=zh|5=湯治|6=ca|7=Termalisme|8=de|9=Thermalbad|10=es|11=Termalismo|13=Thermal bath|14=eo|15=Varmfonta banejo|16=fr|17=Thermalisme|18=la|19=Balneum thermale|20=lb|21=Thermalbad|22=hu|23=Termálfürdő|24=nl|25=Thermaalbad|26=pt|27=Turismo termal|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toji|13=User:Immanuelle/Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}} (healing retreat), Kuroyu offers both a self-catering section and a standard inn, catering to long-term visitors and climbers. Neighboring {{ill|Magoroku Onsen|ja|孫六温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magoroku Onsen|1=Magoroku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11448925}} is a short walk away, connected by a footbridge over the river.

Kuroyu is the only establishment in Nyuto Onsenkyō that closes during the winter, as the other six inns operate year-round.

The onsen inspired {{ill|Yoshiharu Tsuge|en|Yoshiharu Tsuge|WD=Q1353888|lt=Yoshiharu Tsuge|1=Yoshiharu Tsuge}}, a famous manga artist, who visited in May 1969 and September 1976. He depicted its atmosphere in four illustrations titled "Scenery of Travels" and "Tōgenkō." <ref>"[http://www.tsugeyoshiharu.com/makita2.html Traveling with Yoshiharu Tsuge]"</ref>

== Access ==
* Railway
**** Take the JR {{ill|Akita Shinkansen|en|Akita Shinkansen|WD=Q220696|lt=Akita Shinkansen|1=Akita Shinkansen}} to {{ill|Tazawako Station|en|Tazawako Station|WD=Q692529|lt=Tazawako Station|1=Tazawako Station}}, then take the Ugokotsu Bus "Nyuto Line" for approximately 55 minutes to the final stop, followed by a 20-minute walk.
* By car
**** From {{ill|Japan National Route 341|en|Japan National Route 341|WD=Q6157289|lt=Route 341 (Japan)|1=Japan National Route 341}}, take {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 127|ja|秋田県道127号駒ケ岳線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 127|1=Akita Prefectural Road Route 127|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11595557}} and {{ill|Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja|秋田県道194号西山生保内線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefectural Route 194|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩手県道・秋田県道194号西山生保内線|1=Iwate Prefectural Road and Akita Prefectural Road Route 194|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475027}}. At the Nyuto Onsenkyō Holiday Village, turn right and continue 1.3 km to the parking lot near the inn.

**** {{ill|Kaniba Onsen|ja|蟹場温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaniba Onsen|1=Ganiba Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11625863}}
**** {{ill|Ōgama Onsen|ja|大釜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōgama Onsen|1=Ōbuka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439784}}
**** {{ill|Tsuruno-yu Onsen|ja|鶴の湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruno-yu Onsen|qq=}}
